description: The provided image is unreadable and displays only a 'Check for updates' badge icon. Based on the comprehensive text extraction summary and paper context regarding Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel, this Figure likely presents a series of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs or corresponding characterization data showing the evolution of microstructure during cold rolling from 0% to 90% reduction. The as-quenched state (ST) contains equiaxed austenite grains (~70 μm) with annealing twins and nano-sized κ'-carbides formed via spinodal decomposition. Upon cold rolling, the microstructure transitions from Taylor lattice structures with planar slip bands (spacing ~231 nm at 30% reduction) to complex substructures featuring microbands (width ~166 nm) and nano-sized deformation twins (thickness ~5.1 nm) at 50% reduction. At higher reductions (70-90%), twins coarsen to ~19.2 nm, shear bands containing ultrafine grains develop, and dislocation tangles/cells (average size 0.49 μm) form, indicating cross slip activation. This microstructural evolution correlates with the progressive increase in yield strength from ~570 MPa (ST) to 2100 MPa (90% CR), supporting the strengthening mechanisms involving Taylor lattice interactions and Hall-Petch grain refinement.
